questions.question:
Which of the following is/are true of the auto-increment addressing mode?
I. It is useful in creating self-relocating code
II. If it is included in an Instruction Set Architecture, then an additional ALU is required for effective address calculation
III. The amount of increment depends on the size of the data item accessed

A. I only
B. II only
C. III only
D. II and III only
questions.to_find: Which statements about auto-increment addressing mode are true

understand.plain: Auto-increment addressing mode automatically increments the register (used as a pointer) after each memory access. The key question is which of the three given statements about this mode are correct. Statement I claims it helps with self-relocating code (it does not — that is relative addressing). Statement II claims it needs an extra ALU (it does not — a simple incrementer or adder in the register file suffices). Statement III claims the increment amount depends on data size (this is true — byte access increments by 1, word by 2 or 4, etc.).

keywords.explain: An addressing mode where the register holding the effective address is automatically incremented after the memory access by the size of the operand.
keywords.example: If R0 = 1000 and we do LOAD R1, (R0)+, then R1 gets MEM[1000] and R0 becomes 1002 (for a 2-byte word).

keywords.explain: Code that can execute correctly regardless of where it is loaded in memory, typically achieved using relative (PC-relative) addressing.
keywords.example: A branch instruction using PC-relative offset moves to the same relative location regardless of load address.

keywords.explain: The actual memory address computed by the processor for a memory reference instruction.
keywords.example: In auto-increment, the effective address is the current register value before increment.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' width='480' height='200' viewBox='0 0 480 200'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='10' y='10' width='140' height='60' class='hi' rx='6'/><text x='80' y='38' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>Register R0</text><text x='80' y='60' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>value = 1000</text><rect x='200' y='10' width='140' height='60' class='bg' rx='6'/><text x='270' y='38' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>Memory[1000]</text><text x='270' y='60' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>data fetched</text><rect x='10' y='120' width='140' height='60' class='hi' rx='6'/><text x='80' y='148' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>Register R0</text><text x='80' y='170' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>value = 1002 (+2)</text><text x='170' y='45' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>--></text><text x='80' y='108' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>After access (word=2B)</text><line x1='80' y1='70' x2='80' y2='120' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2' marker-end='url(#arr)'/><defs><marker id='arr' markerWidth='8' markerHeight='8' refX='4' refY='4' orient='auto'><path d='M0,0 L8,4 L0,8 Z' fill='#7F77DD'/></marker></defs><text x='340' y='90' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>Increment = size of operand</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Auto-increment: register R0 holds address 1000, memory at 1000 is accessed, then R0 is incremented by operand size (e.g., 2 for word) to become 1002.

given.aim: Determine which statements about auto-increment addressing mode are true.

terms.meaning: Claims auto-increment is useful for self-relocating code.
terms.example: Self-relocating code needs PC-relative addressing, not auto-increment.
terms.connects: This is FALSE — auto-increment just steps through memory sequentially.

terms.meaning: Claims auto-increment requires an additional ALU.
terms.example: Only a simple adder (or dedicated incrementer) in the register file is needed, not a full extra ALU.
terms.connects: This is FALSE — no additional full ALU is required.

terms.meaning: The increment amount equals the size of the data item accessed.
terms.example: Byte access: R increments by 1; word (2B) access: R increments by 2; double-word (4B): by 4.
terms.connects: This is TRUE — the hardware increments by operand size to keep the pointer aligned for the next element.

given.plan: Evaluate each statement against the definition of auto-increment addressing. Statement I is false (self-relocating code uses relative addressing). Statement II is false (no extra ALU needed, just a register increment). Statement III is true (increment size = operand size).

steps.title: Evaluate Statement I
steps.apply: Self-relocating (position-independent) code relies on PC-relative addressing, where branch and data addresses are computed as offsets from the program counter. Auto-increment simply advances a register pointer through consecutive memory locations — it has no relationship to making code position-independent. Statement I is FALSE.
steps.note: Confusing auto-increment with relative addressing is a common trap.

steps.title: Evaluate Statement II
steps.apply: Auto-increment requires adding the operand size to the register after each access. This can be done by a simple dedicated adder or incrementer circuit within the register file or execution unit — it does NOT require an additional full-fledged ALU. Statement II is FALSE.
steps.note: Some architectures implement this with a minimal adder, not a complete ALU.

steps.title: Evaluate Statement III
steps.apply: After accessing a byte, the register increments by 1; for a 2-byte half-word by 2; for a 4-byte word by 4; etc. This ensures the next access points to the next consecutive element of the same type. Statement III is TRUE.
steps.note: This is the defining characteristic of auto-increment — it is especially useful for traversing arrays or stack operations.

solution.result: Only Statement III is true. The correct answer is C (III only).

questions.formula_note: Auto-increment addressing: effective_address = R; then R = R + operand_size. Only Statement III correctly describes this behavior.

questions.question:
Consider a hypothetical processor with an instruction of type LW R1, 20(R2), which during execution reads a 32-bit word from memory and stores it in register R1. The effective address of the memory location is obtained by the addition of a constant 20 and the contents of register R2. Which of the following best reflects the addressing mode implemented by this instruction for the operand in memory?

A. Immediate addressing
B. Register addressing
C. Scaled addressing
D. Base Indexed Addressing
questions.to_find: The addressing mode used in the instruction LW R1, 20(R2)

understand.plain: The instruction LW R1, 20(R2) loads a word from memory. The memory address is computed as the sum of the constant 20 (the displacement/offset) and the content of register R2 (the base register). This is the classic definition of base-displacement (base indexed) addressing, where a base register holds the starting address and a constant offset selects a specific location relative to it.

keywords.explain: The effective address is computed as: EA = Base_Register + Displacement_Constant.
keywords.example: LW R1, 20(R2) means EA = R2 + 20. Commonly used to access struct fields or local variables on the stack.

keywords.explain: A constant embedded in the instruction that is added to a base register to find the effective address.
keywords.example: In LW R1, 20(R2), the displacement is 20.

keywords.explain: The actual memory address used to read or write data.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' width='480' height='180' viewBox='0 0 480 180'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='10' y='20' width='120' height='50' class='hi' rx='6'/><text x='70' y='43' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>R2 = 1000</text><text x='70' y='62' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>(base register)</text><rect x='160' y='20' width='100' height='50' class='bg' rx='6'/><text x='210' y='43' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>20</text><text x='210' y='62' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>(displacement)</text><text x='275' y='48' class='lbl ink'>+</text><rect x='295' y='20' width='130' height='50' class='ok' rx='6'/><text x='360' y='43' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl' style='fill:white'>EA = 1020</text><text x='360' y='62' text-anchor='middle' class='sub' style='fill:white'>Memory address</text><rect x='295' y='110' width='130' height='50' class='hi' rx='6'/><text x='360' y='133' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>R1 = MEM[1020]</text><text x='360' y='152' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>(loaded word)</text><line x1='360' y1='70' x2='360' y2='110' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2' marker-end='url(#arr2)'/><defs><marker id='arr2' markerWidth='8' markerHeight='8' refX='4' refY='4' orient='auto'><path d='M0,0 L8,4 L0,8 Z' fill='#7F77DD'/></marker></defs></svg>
understand.visual_alt: EA = R2 + 20 = 1000 + 20 = 1020. R1 is loaded with the 32-bit word stored at memory address 1020.

given.aim: Identify the addressing mode in LW R1, 20(R2) where EA = R2 + 20.

terms.meaning: Base register holding the starting (base) address.
terms.example: If R2 = 1000, it points to the start of a data structure.
terms.connects: Forms the base component of the effective address calculation.

terms.meaning: Constant displacement (offset) embedded in the instruction.
terms.example: Selects the field 20 bytes past the start of the structure.
terms.connects: Added to R2 to get the final effective address.

terms.meaning: Effective address formula: base register + constant offset.
terms.connects: This formula uniquely identifies the Base Indexed (Base-Displacement) addressing mode.

given.plan: Match the EA computation formula (register + constant) against the definitions of the given addressing modes. Immediate stores the data value in the instruction itself. Register uses only a register, no memory. Scaled multiplies a register by a scale factor. Base Indexed adds a constant to a register — this matches exactly.

steps.title: Identify the EA computation
steps.apply: The instruction computes the memory address as: EA = contents of R2 + constant 20. This involves one register (R2) acting as a base and a constant displacement (20).
steps.note: The constant is part of the instruction encoding, making it a displacement field.

steps.title: Eliminate wrong options
steps.apply:
A. Immediate addressing: the operand value is embedded directly in the instruction — no memory access for the operand. Not applicable here.
B. Register addressing: operand is in a register, no memory reference. Not applicable.
C. Scaled addressing: EA = R_base + (R_index * scale factor). Requires two registers and a scale — not present here.
D. Base Indexed Addressing: EA = Base_Register + Displacement. Matches perfectly.
steps.note: Base Indexed Addressing is also called Base-Displacement or Register-Indirect-with-Offset addressing.

solution.result: The instruction uses Base Indexed Addressing (EA = R2 + 20). Correct answer: D.

questions.formula_note: Base-Displacement (Base Indexed) addressing: EA = Base_Register + Displacement_Constant. Used extensively for struct field access and stack frame variable access.

questions.answer: D. Index addressing mode, with X as an offset represented in 2's complement 16-bit representation
questions.to_find: The most efficient addressing mode to access student.grade given the base address of student in R1

understand.plain: The struct 'data' has an array of 100 integers (marks[100]) followed by a char (grade) and an int (cnumber). Each int is 4 bytes, so marks[100] occupies 400 bytes. The field 'grade' is at an offset of 400 bytes from the start of the struct. Given the base address of student in R1, we need to compute EA = R1 + 400 to access grade. This is exactly what index (base + displacement) addressing does — add a fixed constant offset (400) to a base register (R1).

keywords.explain: The byte distance from the start of a struct to a particular field.
keywords.example: In struct data, marks occupies bytes 0-399, so grade starts at offset 400.

keywords.explain: EA = Base_Register + constant_offset. Ideal for accessing fields of a struct when the base is in a register.
keywords.example: LW R2, 400(R1) loads the value at address R1+400, which is student.grade.

keywords.explain: The constant offset is encoded as a signed 16-bit integer in the instruction, allowing both positive and negative offsets.
keywords.example: 400 in 16-bit 2's complement is 0x0190, which is a valid 16-bit value.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' width='480' height='240' viewBox='0 0 480 240'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='10' y='10' width='200' height='40' class='hi' rx='4'/><text x='110' y='35' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>marks[0..99] — 400 bytes</text><rect x='10' y='55' width='200' height='40' class='ok' rx='4'/><text x='110' y='80' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl' style='fill:white'>grade — 1 byte (offset 400)</text><rect x='10' y='100' width='200' height='40' class='bg' rx='4'/><text x='110' y='125' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>cnumber — 4 bytes</text><rect x='260' y='20' width='120' height='40' class='hi' rx='6'/><text x='320' y='38' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>R1 = base</text><text x='320' y='54' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>start of student</text><text x='245' y='82' class='sub ink'>EA = R1 + 400</text><line x1='380' y1='40' x2='420' y2='75' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2' marker-end='url(#a3)'/><text x='390' y='100' class='sub ink'>--> grade</text><defs><marker id='a3' markerWidth='8' markerHeight='8' refX='4' refY='4' orient='auto'><path d='M0,0 L8,4 L0,8 Z' fill='#1D9E75'/></marker></defs></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Struct layout: marks[0..99] at offset 0-399, grade at offset 400, cnumber at offset 401-404. R1 holds the base address. EA = R1 + 400 accesses grade.

given.aim: Find the addressing mode to access student.grade, which is at offset 400 from the struct base held in R1.

terms.meaning: Array of 100 integers. Each int = 4 bytes, so total = 400 bytes.
terms.example: marks occupies bytes 0 through 399 from the start of student.
terms.connects: Determines the offset of grade = 400 bytes.

terms.meaning: The char grade field starts at byte 400 from the struct base.
terms.connects: This constant offset is the displacement in index addressing.

terms.meaning: Register R1 holds the starting address of the student variable.
terms.example: If R1 = 2000, then student.grade is at address 2400.
terms.connects: Acts as the base register in index (base + displacement) addressing.

given.plan: Compute the offset of grade within the struct (= 100 * 4 = 400 bytes). Use index addressing: EA = R1 + 400. The offset 400 is a fixed constant that fits in a 16-bit 2's complement field, so option D is correct.

steps.title: Compute the offset of student.grade
steps.apply: The struct starts with marks[100]. Since int is 4 bytes, marks occupies 4 * 100 = 400 bytes (indices 0 to 399). The field grade (char, 1 byte) immediately follows at offset 400.
steps.note: Assuming no padding between marks and grade for a typical 32-bit system (or that the question ignores alignment padding for the char field).

steps.title: Identify the addressing mode
steps.apply: Given R1 = base of student, we need EA = R1 + 400 to reach grade. This is index addressing (base register + constant displacement). The constant 400 = 0x190, which fits well within a signed 16-bit representation (range -32768 to +32767). Options A and B (post-increment and pre-decrement) change R1 and do not directly jump to offset 400. Option C (register direct) uses R1 itself as the EA, which points to marks[0], not grade. Option D (index addressing with 16-bit 2's complement offset X = 400) is exactly right.
steps.note: Post-increment/pre-decrement are useful for sequential traversal but cannot efficiently reach an arbitrary field at a known offset.

solution.result: student.grade is at offset 400 from the base. Index addressing with EA = R1 + 400 (offset X = 400, in 16-bit 2's complement) is used. Correct answer: D.

questions.formula_note: Struct field access via Index (Base + Displacement) addressing: EA = Base_Register + constant_offset. Offset of grade = sizeof(int) * 100 = 400 bytes.

questions.question:
Which of the following statements about relative addressing mode is FALSE?

A. It enables reduced instruction size
B. It allows indexing of array element with same instruction
C. It enables easy relocation of data
D. It allows faster effective address calculation than absolute addressing
questions.answer: B. It allows indexing of array element with same instruction
questions.to_find: The FALSE statement about relative addressing mode

understand.plain: Relative addressing mode computes the effective address as EA = PC + offset, where the offset is a signed constant embedded in the instruction. This is commonly used for branch instructions and position-independent code. The question asks which of the four statements about relative addressing mode is NOT true. Indexing array elements typically requires a variable offset (i.e., the array index changes at runtime), which cannot be achieved with a fixed constant offset embedded in the instruction — that requires indexed or base-register addressing.

keywords.explain: EA = Program Counter (PC) + signed offset constant embedded in the instruction.
keywords.example: A branch instruction 'BEQ +12' jumps to PC + 12, regardless of the absolute address.

keywords.explain: Accessing different elements of an array by varying an index variable at runtime.
keywords.example: a[i] requires EA = base + i*size, where i is a runtime variable — this needs indexed/register addressing.

keywords.explain: Code that can execute correctly at any memory location, typically using PC-relative addressing.
keywords.example: Shared libraries use PC-relative calls so they work regardless of load address.

understand.visual_alt: Relative addressing computes EA = PC + fixed_offset. This works for branches and code relocation but cannot vary the offset at runtime, so array indexing (which needs a variable offset) is not directly supported by a single relative-addressing instruction.

given.aim: Identify which statement about relative addressing mode is false.

terms.meaning: Relative offsets are typically shorter (e.g., 8 or 16 bits) than full absolute addresses (32 or 64 bits), so instruction encoding is more compact.
terms.example: A branch offset of 8 bits covers -128 to +127 bytes, needing only 1 byte instead of 4 for a 32-bit absolute address.

terms.term: Statement B: Array element indexing with same instruction
terms.meaning: Claims that relative addressing can index different array elements using the same instruction.
terms.example: For a[0], a[1], a[2], the offset changes with the index — a fixed PC-relative offset cannot do this with the same instruction.
terms.connects: This is FALSE — array indexing requires a variable (runtime) index, not a fixed offset.

terms.meaning: Code using relative addressing can be loaded at any address because addresses are computed relative to PC, not absolute.
terms.example: A shared library relocated to a different address still works if it uses relative addressing throughout.

given.plan: Evaluate each statement: A is true (shorter offsets = smaller instructions). B is false (array indexing needs a variable index, not a fixed relative offset). C is true (relative addressing enables position-independent/relocatable code). D is true (relative addressing computation is simpler than computing a full absolute address or going through a page table).

steps.title: Evaluate statement A
steps.apply: Relative addressing uses a short signed offset (e.g., 8 or 16 bits) rather than a full absolute address (32 or 64 bits). This reduces the instruction size. Statement A is TRUE.
steps.note: This is one of the main advantages of relative addressing — compact branch instructions in ISAs like ARM Thumb.

steps.title: Evaluate statement B
steps.apply: In relative addressing, the offset is a constant encoded in the instruction. To index different array elements (a[0], a[1], a[2], ...), the effective address must vary based on the runtime value of the index variable. A fixed constant offset cannot produce different addresses for different array elements. Array indexing requires indexed addressing (EA = base + index*size) where the index is held in a register. Therefore, Statement B is FALSE.
steps.note: This is the key insight: relative addressing uses a compile-time constant offset; array indexing requires a run-time variable offset.

steps.title: Evaluate statements C and D
steps.apply: C: Relative addressing is the foundation of position-independent code (PIC), allowing data and code to be easily relocated — TRUE. D: EA = PC + offset is a simple addition involving only the PC and a small constant, which is faster than fetching a full 32/64-bit absolute address or computing a virtual-to-physical translation — TRUE.
steps.note: The only FALSE statement is B.

solution.result: Statement B is false — relative addressing uses a fixed offset and cannot directly index array elements with the same instruction. Correct answer: B.

questions.formula_note: Relative addressing: EA = PC + signed_constant_offset. Array indexing requires a variable (runtime) offset, which relative addressing cannot provide with a single fixed-offset instruction.
